claim: LLM-as-a-Judge is a practical evaluation pattern where one LLM compares outputs from two candidate models and returns structured JSON with a winner and reasoning
actionable: To evaluate two models for your use case, create a judge prompt that defines evaluation criteria (correctness, clarity, reasoning, safety), send it both responses, and parse the JSON winner field
source_title: LLM as a Judge Explained | Hands-On GenAI Evaluation with Real Code

claim: Constraining LLM output to structured JSON format (via prompt instruction) is essential for automated evaluation; without structure constraints, outputs are inconsistent and hard to process
actionable: Always include output format specifications in judge prompts (e.g., 'Return only valid JSON with keys: winner, reason'); use JSON.load() to parse and validate programmatically
source_title: LLM as a Judge Explained | Hands-On GenAI Evaluation with Real Code

claim: LLM-as-a-Judge patterns are valuable for evaluating domain-specific AI agents (RAG systems, fine-tuned models) against generic LLMs to identify which performs better for your use case
actionable: When building RAG or specialized AI applications, run multiple candidate models through the same test set and use another LLM as judge to compare quality—this scales better than manual review
source_title: LLM as a Judge Explained | Hands-On GenAI Evaluation with Real Code

claim: Using llama-index wrapper library instead of standalone model libraries (OpenAI, Gemini) makes it easier to swap models without rewriting code
actionable: When building evaluation systems that test multiple models, use llama-index LLM abstractions instead of model-specific SDKs; this lets you test Grok, OpenAI, Gemini interchangeably
source_title: LLM as a Judge Explained | Hands-On GenAI Evaluation with Real Code

claim: Environment variables (via .env files) should be used for API keys rather than hardcoding them; share env template files, not actual credentials
actionable: Create .env files locally for API keys; commit env.template to git showing required variables but not values; this prevents accidental credential exposure
source_title: LLM as a Judge Explained | Hands-On GenAI Evaluation with Real Code

claim: Judge prompts should include explicit evaluation criteria (correctness, completeness, clarity, safety, best practices) to guide consistent and fair model comparisons
actionable: When designing judge prompts, enumerate 4-6 specific criteria you care about and tell the judge to evaluate against those criteria; this prevents arbitrary or inconsistent judgments
source_title: LLM as a Judge Explained | Hands-On GenAI Evaluation with Real Code

claim: Testing domain-specific models (e.g., AWS/DevOps Q&A agents) against generic models using LLM-as-a-Judge reveals which model class better suits your application's needs
actionable: If you've fine-tuned or specialized an LLM, validate improvement by comparing it to generic baselines using LLM-as-a-Judge on your specific domain test set
source_title: LLM as a Judge Explained | Hands-On GenAI Evaluation with Real Code

claim: LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation costs money with paid APIs; be mindful of token usage when evaluating large test sets with multiple models
actionable: When running LLM-as-a-Judge evaluations at scale, calculate expected costs first; test on small subsets before full evaluation runs
source_title: LLM as a Judge Explained | Hands-On GenAI Evaluation with Real Code
